revision.text:
{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Princess Inoe | succession =[[Empress of Japan|Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] * }} }} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|Empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Life == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&pg=PA209&lpg=PA209&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=yXERaADzgb&sig=ACfU3U1cmTwY6smUYgjNOdZC36ZXJqnDTg&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwC3oECAQQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in housearrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&pg=PA146&lpg=PA146&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=a-2Nj9Jh7P&sig=ACfU3U0HJyf-XvGfebhyl1kkkAMxN3nuVQ&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwDXoECAgQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&pg=PA146&lpg=PA146&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=a-2Nj9Jh7P&sig=ACfU3U0HJyf-XvGfebhyl1kkkAMxN3nuVQ&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwDXoECAgQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&pg=PA209&lpg=PA209&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=yXERaADzgb&sig=ACfU3U1cmTwY6smUYgjNOdZC36ZXJqnDTg&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwC3oECAQQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&pg=PA243&dq=empress+Inoue&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjBiurc_ZPiAhWGAxAIHaxhCdYQ6AEILzAB#v=onepage&q=empress%20Inoue&f=false Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
;Issue:
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15#v=onepage&q&f=false ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref>
After the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775, Sakahito married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]], .
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}
{{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{succession box | before= [[Empress Kōmyō]] | title=[[Japanese empresses|Empress consort of Japan]] | after=[[Fujiwara no Otomuro]] | years=770–772}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}}
{{Authority control}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Inoe, Princess}}

;Issue:
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15#v=onepage&q&f=false ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref>

When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&pg=PA243&dq=empress+Inoue&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjBiurc_ZPiAhWGAxAIHaxhCdYQ6AEILzAB#v=onepage&q=empress%20Inoue&f=false Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
;Issue:
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15#v=onepage&q&f=false ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref>
After the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775, Sakahito married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]], .
After the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775, Sakahito married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]].
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Princess Inoe | succession =[[Empress of Japan|Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] * }} }} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|Empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Life == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&pg=PA209&lpg=PA209&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=yXERaADzgb&sig=ACfU3U1cmTwY6smUYgjNOdZC36ZXJqnDTg&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwC3oECAQQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&pg=PA146&lpg=PA146&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=a-2Nj9Jh7P&sig=ACfU3U0HJyf-XvGfebhyl1kkkAMxN3nuVQ&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwDXoECAgQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&pg=PA146&lpg=PA146&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=a-2Nj9Jh7P&sig=ACfU3U0HJyf-XvGfebhyl1kkkAMxN3nuVQ&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwDXoECAgQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&pg=PA209&lpg=PA209&dq=Inoe+empress&source=bl&ots=yXERaADzgb&sig=ACfU3U1cmTwY6smUYgjNOdZC36ZXJqnDTg&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwjU5Pyn_pHiAhXHlIsKHXC-C5cQ6AEwC3oECAQQAQ#v=onepage&q=Inoe%20empress&f=false Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.se/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&pg=PA243&dq=empress+Inoue&hl=sv&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjBiurc_ZPiAhWGAxAIHaxhCdYQ6AEILzAB#v=onepage&q=empress%20Inoue&f=false Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
;Issue:
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15#v=onepage&q&f=false ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref>
After the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775, Sakahito married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]].
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{DEFAULTSORT:Inoe}} [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:717 births]] [[Category:775 deaths]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese women]] [[Category:8th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:Saigū]] [[Category:Deified Japanese women]] [[Category:Daughters of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:8th-century empresses consort]]

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan|''Kōshitsu'']] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|Empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Life == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.
==Notes== {{Reflist}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.

During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>

In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>

[[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.

In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

(1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.

*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. revision.text:
{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

(1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.

[[Category:Uncategorized pages]] [[Category:tt]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]] [[Category:tt]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Princess Inoe]][[Category:Uncategorized pages]] [[Category:tt]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[en:Princess Inoe[[Category:kk]] [[Category:kkkk]]

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>

When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

Inoe[[Category:kk]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]]

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

[https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. == Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

Inoe[[Category:kk]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:pages of uncertain originality]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 276–277; Varley, H. Paul. ''Jinnō Shōtōki,'' pp. 147–148; Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). {{Google books|18oNAAAAIAAJ|''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' pp. 81–85.|page=81}}</ref> She was deposed in 772, accused of witchcraft.
== Biography == Inoue was the daughter of [[Emperor Shōmu]], who reigned from March 3, 724 until August 19, 749.<ref name="kunaicho">[[Imperial Household Agency]] (''Kunaichō''): [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/045/index.html 聖武天皇 (45)]</ref> Her mother was [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] (県犬養広刀自), daughter of [[Agatainukai no Morokoshi]]. She was the sister of Prince Asaka (d. 744) and Princess Fuwa, and the half sister of [[Empress Kōken]].
Princess Inoue married her relative, the future Emperor Kōnin, thereby uniting the ''Tenmu'' and ''Tenji'' line of the Imperial House. During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

During the last reign of her half sister Empress Kōken (764-770), Inoue and her sister Fuwa where both involved in political plots with ambition to the succession of the throne: Fuwa wished to place her husband and sons on the throne, while Inoue wished to have her son Osabe appointed Crown Prince. <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
==Issue==
*Imperial Prince Osabe (d. 775), the Crown Prince *[[Princess Sakahito]]<ref>Goethem, Ellen Van (2008). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XpyR7KHkvc0C&pg=PA15 ''Nagaoka: Japan's forgotten capital'']. [[Brill Publishers]] via [[Google Books]]</ref> married her elder half-brother [[Emperor Kanmu|Yamabe, later Kanmu]] after the deaths of her mother and Osabe in 775.
== Legacy == [[File:五條市霊安寺町 御霊神社 Goryō-jinja, Ryōanji-chō 2011.3.31 - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Goryō Jinja in Gojō, Nara]] Around 800, during the reign of [[Kammu Tenno|Kammu]], former Prince Yamabe, a shrine was built for her in Yamashiro Province (now [[Gojō, Nara]]), named Goryō Jinja. Princess Inoe is also venerated at its subsideries.

{{Infobox royalty | consort =yes | name =Inoe | succession =[[Empress consort of Japan]] | reign = 770 – 772 | birth_date = 717 | birth_place = | death_date = 775 | death_place = |royal house = [[Imperial House of Japan]] | spouse = [[Emperor Kōnin]] | father = [[Emperor Shōmu]] | mother = [[Agatainukai no Hirotoji]] | issue = {{plainlist| * Imperial Prince Osabe * [[Princess Sakahito]] }}}} '''Princess Inoe''' or ''Inoue'' (717–775) was the [[Japanese empresses|empress consort]] of [[Emperor Kōnin]] of [[Japan]].<ref>Brown and Ishida. In 770, Inoue's spouse succeeded her half sister to the throne due to a fabricated will, after which Inoue was named Empress and her son named Crown Prince. In 772, Empress Inoue was suddenly deposed and arrested and accused for having used curses and black magic to promote her son to the throne, and shortly afterward, her son Prince Osabe was also arrested for complicity. Both Inoue and Osabe was stripped of their titles, banished and imprisoned in house arrest, for having performed curses and practising black magic. <ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> In 775, Inoue and her son both died in custody on the same day, presumably poisoned.<ref>Karl Heinrich Menges, Nelly Naumann, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=3oGLez4d-ncC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA146 Language and Literature: Japanese and the Other Altaic Languages : Studies ...]''</ref> <ref>Herman Ooms, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=8fzx6eRzf-AC&dq=Inoe+empress&pg=PA209 Imperial Politics and Symbolics in Ancient Japan: The Tenmu Dynasty, 650-800]''</ref>
When the Emperor fell ill in 777, Inoue was believed to haunt him, and consequently, she was reinterred in an Imperial grave and rehabilitated; in 800, she was given back her title of Empress posthumously.<ref>Ellen van Goethem, ''[https://books.google.com/books?id=1t6wCQAAQBAJ&dq=empress+Inoue&pg=PA243 Nagaoka: Japan's Forgotten Capital]''</ref>
